MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against relevant prior work that also uses gaze/text features (Mishra et al. 2016c for sentiment and Mishra et al. 2016b for sarcasm) and uses the same datasets and a 10‑fold train/test configuration for fairness (see "Comparison with existing work" paragraph). Results report clear numeric comparisons (e.g. "outperform traditional systems by a maximum margin of 11.27%" and note that adding gaze gives only a 1.36% (insignificant) gain), and the authors acknowledge when text-only models capture the signal well. However, the comparisons omit other modern neural baselines or broader relevant recent work beyond the cited handcrafted-feature systems, and the analysis of differences is somewhat limited to high-level observations rather than deeper ablations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies an existing technique (CNNs) to learn cognitive features from eye-movement/gaze data for sentiment and sarcasm classification, replacing prior manual feature engineering (Mishra et al. 2016) with an automated representation (Sections 3–4). The problem itself (sentiment/sarcasm classification) is well-studied, and CNN architectures and training choices are standard, so the technical approach is an expected adaptation rather than a fundamentally new method. The paper clearly differentiates from prior handcrafted-gaze work but reports only modest gains from the gaze component (e.g., a 1.36% increase for sarcasm), indicating moderate, incremental novelty rather than a surprising or highly original contribution.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (sentiment and sarcasm detection) and introduces a promising direction—automatic learning of cognitive features from gaze data via CNNs rather than manual feature engineering. The method shows notable gains over traditional handcrafted systems (up to 11.27% for sarcasm) and claims improved performance when combining text and gaze features, but the concrete benefit of the gaze component is modest (only a 1.36% gain for sarcasm, statistically insignificant) and experiments rely on small datasets (994 and 843 snippets) with few readers (7 and 5), limiting generalizability. Overall this is a useful, actionable contribution that opens further work on learned cognitive signals in NLP, but its immediate influence is moderate given dataset and effect-size constraints.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses a clear NLP/ML topic—multimodal cognitive signals for sentiment and sarcasm classification—using neural methods (CNNs) that are squarely within top-tier venue scope. It targets the right audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in multimodal and cognitive-NLP) and formulates standard classification tasks (sentiment, sarcasm) with appropriate datasets enriched with eye-tracking (Mishra et al., Joshi et al.). Methods and evaluation follow expected standards (CNN variants, pre-trained embeddings, dropout, ADADELTA, 10-fold train/test splits and comparisons to existing handcrafted-feature baselines), and the work is positioned within the cognitive NLP/multimodal learning literature cited in the paper.

CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is organized in a standard way (intro/motivation, method, experiments, results—e.g. sections 3, 4, 5, 6) but the main contributions are only implicitly stated rather than clearly enumerated early (the claim "We introduce a framework..." appears but is not presented as a concise contribution list). Notation is mostly defined (filter widths H, 2D filters J2, pooling M2) but some phrasing is awkward and occasionally unclear (e.g. "embedding layer with of pre-trained embeddings", "Our systems’s accuracy"). Figures and tables are referenced (Figure 2, Figure 3, Table 2) which suggests useful illustrations, but those references in the excerpt feel relied upon to explain ideas and their absence here reduces clarity. Minor grammatical/formatting issues (missing spaces, possessive errors) and some terse explanations of variants/hyperparameters make parts of the methods and experiments harder to follow.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and provides experiment details (datasets, CNN variants, training hyperparameters) and reports gains (e.g. up to 11.27% over traditional systems and a 1.36% marginal gain from gaze for sarcasm). However, key methodological details are missing or under-specified in the provided text: preprocessing/representation of gaze into the 2D input, exact CNN architecture, and which statistical test was used to assert (in)significance. The datasets are small and gaze comes from only 5–7 readers, raising potential overfitting/generalization concerns, and the motivation for applying 2D convolutions to gaze data is asserted but not justified theoretically. Overall the experimental evidence partly supports the claims but some technical gaps and insufficient reporting prevent a fully convincing validation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs experiments on two public gaze-enriched datasets and evaluates multiple CNN variants (text-only, gaze-only, and combined) with comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature systems and a consistent 10-fold setup (Sections 5.1–5.2, 5.7). It reports many training hyperparameters (filter sizes, number of filters, dropout, optimizer, epochs, embeddings) and some ablation-like variants, but hyperparameters were set by “trial and error” with no systematic sensitivity analysis and tuning is deferred to future work (Section 5.3). The authors note a key negative result — adding gaze gave only a 1.36% (statistically insignificant) gain for sarcasm — but broader failure modes and limitations (e.g., when gaze helps, per-class breakdowns, statistical tests, or how gaze signals are encoded) are not fully analyzed. Reproducibility is partially supported by many parameter choices and dataset citations, but important implementation details (exact gaze representation/preprocessing, architecture diagram/layer-by-layer specs, random seeds, and full result tables) are missing, limiting exact replication.
